text:
Membrane science and technology has entered an era where new, advanced membrane materials are required, to meet a wide range of performance characteristics including productivity, chemical stability and tailored function. Rare earth element (REE) separation is another emerging theme, highly relevant to the needs of society and the high tech industry, which is increasingly demanding for many materials. Examples include permanent magnets for wind turbines and electric motors for hybrid and battery electric vehicles. As such, the demand for REEs, including the individual and mixed metal halide salts used in current solvent extraction processes, has dramatically increased, which is particularly timely, as isolation of REEs is included in the list of the seven most effective processes for improvement in the chemical separation operations that will have positive effects for the whole world.

Membrane technology has rapidly evolved to become one of the popular and green approaches for metal recovery and wastewater treatment. Recent literature showed the large number of methodologies developed for the individual REE separation. A review focusing on the non-conventional liquid membrane approaches is still required. In fact, liquid membranes were the first ones utilised, but most of the times, the stability, membrane durability and extractant leaching have constituted a challenge. To overcome these drawbacks, recent works focused on non-liquid membrane approaches where the carriers are fixed or covalently bonded to the membrane or support material. In this way, the carriers are retained on the membrane and the extractant leakage in the aqueous phase is avoided.

This review summary focuses on the current membrane-based separation approaches for REEs and highlights the potential of imprinted, polymer inclusion, nanocomposite membranes as well as framework-based materials such as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs). The key performance metrics considered are extraction efficiency, mass transport, and membrane stability.

prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
